Find out more in the session catalog for Sapphire Madrid, May 26-28: Product and User Experience Track (6 talks).

 

More Information

I hope to see you either in Orlando or Madrid – feel free to approach me and ask questions!

In case you missed my recent series of blog posts about our recent UX innovations, do have a look, it starts with this one, which contains links to all the other six posts in the series:

SAP User Experience Q1/2025 Update – Part 1: Many New Innovations Available (AI, Joule and More).

entry.title: Optimize SAP ECC Licenses and User Classification Independently with Accelerator for SAM4U
entry.content:
Introduction

Many SAP customers find value in the extended reporting capabilities provided by the Accelerator for SAM4U Adoption (available on the SAP Store) - especially for S/4HANA license management and optimization. However, what about customers who are still operating SAP ECC and plan to do so for several more years? These organizations are still subject to SAP audits and need effective tools for managing and optimizing their SAP ECC licenses.

Let’s explore how Accelerator for SAM4U can also support SAP ECC customers in preparing for SAP audits and optimizing their current license usage.

Analyze Current Classification vs. Authorization-Based Classification

There is no direct mapping between SAP ECC named user license metrics and S/4HANA ruleset metrics. Still, it’s crucial to focus on high-cost license types, such as Professional and Limited Professional users, to unlock potential savings.

For example, in one customer case, the SAP ECC license usage looked like this:

  • 52 Professional Users

  • 53 Limited Professional Users

Using the Authorization Analysis feature of SAM4U’s extended reporting and simulation, we can map users' current manual classifications against their actual usage potential - based on their roles and profiles in the SAP ECC system. This gives insight into what kind of system footprint a user is authorized to leave, aligning more closely with S/4HANA’s licensing logic.

Example: Reclassifying Professional Users

 Pic. 1 – Current Classification vs. Authorization-Based Classification

Let’s focus on the 52 Professional Users:

  • Only 101 users truly have professional roles (mapped to the HB Professional User metric in S/4HANA).

  • 126 users currently classified as Professional Users have roles corresponding only to an HC Functional footprint. These users can be considered, roughly, as 53 Limited Professional Users.

Instead of analyzing each user in the HC Functional group in detail (which is time-consuming), the extended reporting for SAM4U dashboard allows you to drill down and extract a list of these 126 users with a single click. This list can then be sent to your SAP system administrator to update their license metric accordingly.

 Pic. 2 – Users to Reclassify from 52 Professional to 53 Limited Professional

Additionally, there are 184 users classified as 52 Professional Users whose assigned roles do not match any classification in the S/4HANA ruleset.

For these, we recommend mapping them to 59 Core Users - a more cost-effective metric in SAP ECC for unclassified users.

 Pic. 3 – Users to Reclassify from 52 Professional to 59 Core

This rough reclassification already opens up a path to significant license cost savings by reducing the number of users under the expensive 52 Professional Users metric.

Identify Missing Upgrades: Core Users Who Should Be Professional

The final step in this license "homework" is to identify users who are underclassified - those listed as 59 Core Users in SAP ECC, but whose roles match HB Professional in the S/4HANA ruleset.

These users may need to be upgraded to 52 Professional Users to ensure compliance and avoid audit risk. Again, this can be done easily through the drill-down functionality in SAM4U’s reporting dashboard.

 Pic. 4 – Users to Reclassify from 59 Core to 52 Professional

Conclusion

The extended reporting and simulation provided by the Accelerator for SAM4U offers powerful, independent insights into your SAP ECC user classifications. By comparing current user classifications with authorization-based projections (using S/4HANA rulesets), you can:

  • Optimize license allocation

  • Identify over- and under-classified users

  • Prepare confidently for SAP audits

  • Uncover significant cost-saving opportunities

You can request free demo access to the Accelerator for SAM4U Adoption tenant from SAP Store to explore these features and validate your own SAP ECC user classification strategy.

entry.title: SAPUI5 is Leading the Way in Accessibility with WCAG 2.2
entry.content:
The WCAG 2.2 Standard

Accessibility is key to using SAP's business solutions. Adopting the latest standards gives applications a true cutting edge, ensuring that solutions are inclusive and meet the diverse needs of users globally. For meeting the latest Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG), the UI5 team, designers, and SAP's accessibility experts met the challenge to give us that edge with customers.

The latest version WCAG 2.2 introduced new criteria which need to be implemented on both, the UI technology side as well as application side. Some of the new requirements are targeting application logic, e.g. to avoid asking the user for redundant data, to use a consistent navigation, or to offer alternatives for drag and drop. Application developers follow updated guidelines, the UI5 recommendations and the UI5 Accessibility guide. A big portion of the requirements was handled by the central UI technology SAPUI5.

See two typical new requirements to understand the WCAG benefits:

1) The standard describes the minimum target size of 24x24 px for an interactive element, so that users doesn't accidentally activate a different element:

Visualization of the target size of a UI element

2) Each operation which can be performed by dragging can also be performed by using one pointer actions without dragging: 

Context menu with option to maximize a section

WCAG 2.2 and SAPUI5 / UI5 Web Components

SAPUI5 1.136 and UI5 Web Components 2.9.0 adjusted their implementation towards the latest WCAG 2.2 standard. This milestone reflects the unwavering commitment to accessibility and ensures that applications built with UI5 can meet the latest industry standards and customer expectations. Accessibility is a key requirement not only for public sector clients but many other customers. Offering implementations towards WCAG 2.2 fulfills customer expectations and strengthens the competitive position of SAP products.

This is one step in the continuous journey to enhance the user experience and accessibility of SAP solutions. The UI5 team remains dedicated to advancing accessibility features that empower all users.

The ARIA 1.2 Standard

Supporting WCAG, the Accessible Rich Internet Applications (ARIA) standard is used to make Web applications more accessible: It especially helps with dynamic content and advanced UI controls. ARIA adds role, property, and state information to dynamic Web applications to make UI elements recognizable by assistive technologies such as screen readers.

SAPUI5 1.136 and UI5 Web Components 2.9.0 are now implemented towards the latest ARIA 1.2 specification. The UI5 team has worked to ensure these enhancements reflect the best practices for accessible design, thereby helping to improve the overall user experience of consuming applications. This advancement shows how UI5 continues to meet modern standards for accessible user interfaces.

Reference Test Environment

In this context the UI5 team also updated the reference test environment. This ensures compatibility with new technologies and allows making use of the latest innovations. For details see SAP Note 2564165.

References

  • What's New in WCAG 2.2

  • SAPUI5 Accessibility Guide
  • Accessibility in Walkthrough tutorial (JavaScript)
  • Accessibility in Walkthrough tutorial (TypeScript)
  • SAPUI5 essentials: Accessibility
  • SAPUI5 Accessibility Guide for developing apps
  • SAPUI5 Accessibility for End Users
  • Accessibility in UI5 Web Components
  • Accessibility Mission
  • SAP Note 2967114 - Assistive Tool Support of Web-Technology based SAP Products

entry.title: ​​​Determining Service attributes from the Service organization​ ​
entry.content:
1. Determination of Planning Plant from the Enterprise Service Org. 

Overview: 

When the system is enabled with the Enterprise Organizational Model, the planning plant in the Advanced execution order is mandatory in the Service transactions. It was earlier updated manually. Now this can be defined in the customization of the Service organization definition. This allows the determination planning plant for execution items within service transactions from the Service organization. In contrast, under the Legacy Organizational Model, the planning plant is determined directly from the service organization unit or entered manually. 

 How to set up a planning plant in the Service organization? 

  • Step 1.  Configure the planning plant in SPRO. 

         Navigate the following Path in SPRO 

         SPRO -> Enterprise Structure -> Definition -> Service -> Define Enterprise Service Organization. 

  • Step 2. Create a Service order with enterprise service organization. During the process, the planning plant is automatically determined from the Service organization. 

 2. Determination of SLA profiles from the Service organization 

 Overview. 

When the enterprise organization is enabled, the SLA profile can be defined within the enterprise structure at the service level. These profiles are automatically determined at the Service transaction items once the service organization is updated, following the access sequence configured in the backend. 

 How to setup SLA profiles. 

  • Step 1. Path: SPRO-> Service -> Transactions-> Setting for Service Transactions -> Define Availability and Response Time. 

 

  • Step 2. Maintain the service profile and response profile in services under enterprise structure. 

         Path: SPRO -> Enterprise Structure -> Definition -> Service -> Define Enterprise Service Organization. 

  • Step 3. Maintain the SLA determination procedures and access sequence. 

          Path: SPRO -> Services -> Transactions -> Setting for service requests -> Define SLA determination                  procedures for service transactions. 

 

 

  • Step 4.  Assign the SLA profile determination procedure for the transaction types. 

Path: SPRO -> Service -> Transactions -> Define Transaction types -> Selection the transaction types.  

Under assignment of Business transaction categories, select services order/quotations, assign the SLA profile in customizing header. 

  • Step 5.  Create the service order, once the enterprise org is determined, SLA profiles will get determined according to the access sequence.  

Note: SLA profiles will get determined only when both the profiles are maintained in the source object. 

 3.Determine Main Work center & Main work center plant from the Service team 

Overview: 

Main work center and Main work center plant are mandatory for the Advanced execution items in the Service orders. Already there is a logic to determine the Service team based on the Service organization as the Service org is one of the attributes in the Service team. 

Now the Service team has been enhanced with 2 new attributes: the main work center and the main work centre plant. Once the service team is determined / updated in the Service items, the main work centre, and its plant for advanced execution items in service orders will be determined. 

How to set up Service Team with Main work center and Main work center plant. 

  • Step 1. Enter the main work center and main work center plant in the responsibility definition of the service team. 

  • Step 2. Create the service order. Once the service team is determined in the service order, the main work center and main work center plant will be automatically derived from the responsibility definition. 

 4. Enhance the Service Team determination via Enterprise Organization. 

Overview: 

When the enterprise service organization is enabled, the service team is determined based on the enterprise service organization setup. 

How to set up service team determination. 

  •  Go to Service -> Master data -> Define Service Team Determination Rules  
  •  Select Condition -> Assign Attributes. 

 Select the ENTERPRISESERVICEORGANIZATION as responsibility definition in the attribute. 

  • Attributes defined within the responsibility configuration will guide the system in determining the appropriate service team in a service order, using a combination of those attributes. 

 For more information on creation of teams:   

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/responsibility-management-create-your-teams-2-5/ba-p/13733021 

For information on creation of service teams: 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/enterprise-service-organization-service-team-management/ba-p/14072189 

 5. Enhance Flexibility of Spare Part Plant and Storage Location in Definition and Determination. 

 Overview:  

To increase flexibility in defining service organizations, the fields for service part plant and storage location are now optional in Enterprise Service Organizations. This change allows for a more adaptable sequence when determining the appropriate service part plant and storage location. 

The determination sequence is as follows: 

  1. Service Team 
  2. Service Employee + Country/Region/Sales Org/Distribution Channel 
  3. Enterprise Service Organization 
  4. Country/Sales Org/Distribution Channel 

Since the plant and storage location were mandatory in the enterprise organization definition, the system would not reach step 4. With this update, making the service part plant and storage location optional allows the system to go further in the determination sequence, increasing operational flexibility.

entry.title: Enhancing Data Integration and Collaboration through Asset Administration Shell in SAP BNAC
entry.content:
What is SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration (BNAC)?

 SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration (BNAC) solution offers a common foundation for collaborative asset management across different stakeholders involved in the lifecycle of physical assets—such as machinery, equipment, or infrastructure. By utilizing digital twins of these assets, SAP BNAC enhances collaboration among component suppliers, OEMs, and asset operators. This improves resilience, transparency, and operational reliability throughout the entire asset lifecycle. It also extends SAP S/4HANA for B2B integration of asset data and maintenance, using features from SAP Intelligent Asset Management and SAP Fieldglass solutions.

More information about SAP BNAC can be found here.

 

What are Digital Twins and why we need standards?

Simply speaking, digital twins can be seem as virtual representations of physical assets, systems, or processes. By mimicking real characteristics and behaviors of its physical counterparts during its lifecycle, digital twins provide numerous opportunities for business to provide insights into performance, maintenance needs, and enhance overall efficiency.

To fully exploit the potential of digital twins, data is collected from a variety of sensors and data sources with its own formats and data models. While this process partly occurs within the boundaries of a single company, it often involves numerous stakeholders throughout the product lifecycle. In this context, standards are crucial for digital twins to ensure consistency, interoperability, and seamless integration across different platforms and industries. This is where Asset Administration Shell (AAS) becomes significant.

 

Introducing Asset Administration Shell (AAS)

The Asset Administration Shell (AAS) is an industry standard (IEC 63278) for digital twins and driven by industrial leaders under the Industrial Digital Twin Association (IDTA). It provides standardized meta model and APIs allowing both unambiguous identification, representation of physical assets as well as interoperable integration to systems (data sources) relevant for digital twins. One of the features of the AAS is the concept of Submodels. Submodels organizes digital twin data following a specific use case or domain. These Submodels can adhere to established standards such as ECLASS and VDI 2770, ensuring interoperability across different systems, allowing for the seamless sharing and interaction of digital twin data. Well known examples of Submodels are Digital Nameplate and Handover Documentation.

Example of an Asset Administration Shell (AAS) of a robot with Submodels.

  Figure 1 Example of an Asset Administration Shell (AAS) of a robot with Submodels.

 

Asset Administration Shell (AAS) support in SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration (BNAC)

By providing support to the AAS, SAP BNAC establishes an interoperability foundation for component suppliers, OEMs, and operators, facilitating the use of digital twins in collaboration processes. The new feature will enable users to utilize AAS to create corresponding business objects in SAP BNAC and integrate them into business processes via SAP S/4HANA.

High-level overview of collaborative asset management leveraging AAS.

 Figure 2 High-level overview of collaborative asset management leveraging AAS.

  • Seamless and Interoperable Data Import:
    SAP BNAC now supports the effortless import of AAS files (AASX) through its user interface. This allows for the integration of Submodels like the Digital Nameplate and Handover Documentation to create Equipment objects. This standardization simplifies data handling and ensures consistency.
  • Integration with SAP S/4HANA:
    The AAS import functionality translates AAS data into standard SAP objects, integrating them into SAP Plant Maintenance and SAP Material Master. This integration ensures that asset data is not only accurate but also easily accessible across the SAP ecosystem.
  • Extended Partner Collaboration:
    SAP BNAC extends its collaborative capabilities beyond the SAP ecosystem. It ensures interoperability with data ecosystems like Factory-X, enabling businesses to engage with a broader network of partners and service providers.

Figure 2 shows the use case leveraging AAS for asset management with SAP BNAC. In this use case, AAS can be used to transport data of digital twins from other data sources (e.g., engineering system, manufacturing system, etc.) into SAP BNAC. Alternatively, the user could also obtain the AAS from a supplier. With the new feature, SAP BNAC creates the corresponding business object by importing the AAS and allows their integration into SAP S/4HANA. Once the business objects are created, the user can share them with their business partners using SAP BNAC.

A robot manufacturer provides a digital twin of their products to their customers (operators). Typically, the manufacturer spends considerable time searching for component data from suppliers and manually maintaining this information in their SAP system. With SAP BNAC's new functionality, manufacturers can automatically create an Equipment with corresponding Documents by importing an AAS Instance that includes Submodels such as Digital Nameplate and Handover Documentation. The manufacturer can then use existing SAP BNAC features to share the Equipment and Documents with their customer (operator). The operator can integrate the received Equipment and Documents into their SAP S/4HANA system, facilitating additional business processes like maintenance planning.

 

Begin Your Journey in Standardized Digital Twins with the SAP BNAC 2504 Release

Starting with 2504 release, SAP BNAC will enable the import of an AAS file (AASX) containing an AAS Instance with Submodel Digital Nameplate and Handover Documentation through the user interface to create Equipment objects and the corresponding Documents.

Learn more about this feature: https://url.sap/x7hntw

Below are some of screenshots showing the new release. With the feature “Data Import” (Figure 4-7), users can now upload an AASX file (as shown Figure 3). Figure 8-10 shows the resulting Equipment and its Document as derived from AAS.

Figure 3 AASX file with the AAS Instance of a Safety Valve from LESER as modeled with Eclipse AASX Package Explorer from IDTA.

Figure 4 SAP BNAC initial UI with Data Import Feature highlighted.

Figure 5 AASX Import - Part 1: User click on "New" to import AAX File.

Figure 6 AASX Import - Part 2: Users select corresponding AASX file from AAS Instance of LESER Safety Valve as shown above.

Figure 7 AASX Import - Part 3: SAP BNAC processes the AASX file and generates the corresponding Equipment, Attributes and Documents accordingly.

Figure 8 AASX Import – Part 4: Resulting Equipment as derived from AASX file.

Figure 9 AASX Import - Part 5: Equipment Data Sheet containing the Attributes as derived form AAS Submodel Digital Nameplate.

Figure 10 AASX Import - Part 6: Documents derived from AAS Submodel Handover Documentation.

 

Conclusion

The 2504 release of SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration (BNAC) marks a significant milestone in the realm of collaborative asset management. By supporting Asset Administration Shell (AAS), SAP BNAC offers an effortless and standardized way to import, integrate, and manage asset data as digital twins. This enhancement ensures that businesses can capitalize on the full potential of digital twins, fostering a more collaborative and efficient environment. Here is an overview of the value proposition:

  • Automatic Asset Data Creation:  Saves time and money by avoiding manual errors.
  • Easy Data Sharing: Allows manufacturers, operators, and service providers to focus on their business processes.
  • Integrate industry standards into your business processes: Synchronize digital twins with SAP S/4HANA (Plant Maintenance and Material Master).
  • Future-proof: Getting ready for Digital Product Passport (DPP) and collaborating in Factory-X.

 

Join us and be part of the Digital Twin Ecosystem

We are pleased to introduce this innovative feature and invite you to assist us in refining and perfecting it. Your feedback is invaluable as we enhance the AAS support in SAP BNAC. Join us in driving the future of digital twin technology.

Please contact us today (email ) to learn how SAP BNAC can transform your business operations.

At SAP, we are dedicated to more than just software development—we are pioneering the next wave of industrial innovation. Harness the power of digital twins to elevate your business to new heights.

This is only the beginning—additional enhancements are forthcoming as we continue to broaden our support for industry standards and cross-system connectivity. Stay tuned!

Further information:

  • SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration
  • SAP Help Portal - What's New in SAP BNAC
  • SAP Help Porta - Data Import (AAS Import)

entry.title: Project Kernseife - improving the measurement of Clean Core in ABAP
entry.content:
TL;DR

To improve the handling and measuring of classic ABAP code in ATC with regards to clean core, the possibility of an ATC check based on a custom classification was developed and is now published as an open source project named "Project Kernseife".

 

Challenges in Measuring Clean Core

Working in the area of clean core - especially focusing on the dimension "extensibility" - in projects of different scales, we were more than once asked in the last years on how to measure and govern the clean core principles within SAP S/4HANA systems (focusing on Private Cloud & On-Premises).

A key part to describe the "clean core state" of extensions is from our point of view the creation of a technical debt score that should align with the governance process in your system. That is why SAP's own code quality tool ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC) is very helpful on this. Since the release of "ABAP Cloud" as target for On-Stack extensions, we have now possibibilities with the related ATC Check Variant (ABAP_CLOUD_READINESS) to identify the "distance to Tier 1".

But looking at landscapes with large amount of legacy code (e.g. > 100,000 custom code objects in classic ABAP) or systems where you cannot use sufficiently released APIs for Tier 1, it becomes more difficult to identify the "real" technical debt.

If you run with the default ABAP_CLOUD_READINESS variant into larger systems, you might be overwhelmed by a huge number of findings in ATC - that are all marked as Error. This is logical, if you want to identify the criticality as "what do I need to change to get into ABAP Cloud", but at the same time the important technical debt might be hidden behind a lot of "false positive" findings.

To improve this situation, SAP released the new 3 Tier check (SAP Note 3565942) in February of 2025. This check improves the situation by analyzing "classic" coding with a stronger focus on technical debt, to identify the "right" things. It is based on SAP's released objects (Tier 1, official APIs) and nominated Classic APIs. These Classic APIs belong either to business logic (e.g. BAPI_PO_CREATE1) or a technical framework (e.g. ALV). Finally, there are now also "noAPIs" (objects that must not be used, e.g. RFC_READ_TABLE) nominated by SAP.

Still - we saw a gap in projects to improve the lifes of lead developers: A custom classification. Because SAP may not be able to classify a certain ABAP object as precise as you may be able to do. So SAP is not publishing any information on a given ABAP object and it is shown as accessing an internal object (which leads in ATC to a warning). But in your use case, you are fine with using it. And this is where Kernseife can help you.

 

What is "Kernseife"?

Kernseife consists of two parts:

  1. A custom ATC Check that is based on the new Tier 3 check mentioned above, but uses a custom classification system.
  2. CAP Application to create a custom classification of ABAP objects (and we even use it to analyze results from systems in a simple way).

ATC Check for custom classification

As mentioned, we started to work on "Kernseife" in 2024 as there was no detailed Tier 3 check available then. Therefore, our focus was initially to create a custom ATC check that can work with a custom classification. It is now re-worked and based on the "new" ATC Check (Note 3565942).

This check is basically analyzing your custom code like the Tier 3 check, but when it comes to the evaluation of the impact of a finding, the custom classification comes into play. You can upload the custom classification as a JSON file and in this classification will then used to determine your findings. Let's explain the difference with an example:

In your project, you need to work with the RF framework, because you are implementing EWM in your Private Cloud Environment. Therefore, you need to work with the classes /SCWM/CL_RF*, that are the recommended way to interact with the RF framework. If you look into the Cloudifcation Repostory, you can see that these classes are not released or nominated as "classic API". Still, you want to generally accept the usage of the RF framework for both: Governance of developers (ATC Checks) and measuring your "distance to clean core".

By using your custom classification which contains the classes /SCWM/CL_RF* as "Business Function - Recommended" (BF1), which will lead to only "Information" (Prio 3) findings in the check and will have a lower impact on the "clean core score", you can improve your efficiency.

For details on the custom classification, you can check the classification guide.

Application to manage Classifications

Of course, you could maintain the JSON file of the classification manually based on the given json format. But we created a little app, that is currently in a feature branch, to do that in a more sophisticated way.

In this application, you can initially check the current state for objects as defined by SAP and additionally add objects that are not yet mentioned by SAP.

Sticking with the example from above, you can now edit the "Rating Code" of the /SCWM/CL_RF* classes to "BF1". After you did your changes, you can download the classification JSON file, upload it into your ATC check system and all of the findings for these classes will in future be recorded as "Information" (Prio 3) message.

 

Governance and Measurability

With this custom classification and the usage in ABAP Test Cockpit, we can now combine the governance view and the measurability within ATC.

Governance

By enhancing SAP's default rating, you can control your developers more efficiently. Because as of today, your lead developers that are able to approve exemptions might be overwhelmed by a lot of "false positive" ATC findings. Just imagine every usage of the example classes "/SCWM/CL_RF*" need to be exempted.

In projects - especially looking at the overwhelming errors from the "ABAP_CLOUD_READINESS" ATC check in classic ABAP, you can now control with your custom classification, that recurring "false positive" findings will disappear (or become prio 3 messages only).

This is important, as - being a reviewer - your chances to ask questions on the important findings will improve. Because if for every major developments you need to approve hundreds of exemption requests, you will most probably go into "autopilot" approval mode as you cannot check all of the requests in detail.

Measurability

Our second motivation to create this check is to create a more realistic impact rating to measure what we call the "Kernseife Score" (basically: a technical debt score per object). With every classification (BF1), we consider a score of how severe the impact of using this SAP object is with regards to clean core.

So you can configure now a score - we did use a 0 to 10 scale - to identify how severe is a single finding. Based on the ATC check, we can now provide per object a score that you can use for your reporting. Either to identify the total score per system, per package, per object - or even to deep dive into analyses like "which object has the worst average score per finding?" or "which object is in total view the "uncleanest" object in my system?".

While you can then easily use your analytics tools (e.g. SAP Analytics Cloud) to create the dashboards you need, in our repository you can at least find a CDS-View you can use for extraction (ZKNSF_I_SCORING), that combines the findings with the score. If you want to directly extract e.g. a list aggregated by object, you could directly use another CDS-View (ZKNSF_I_DEVELOPMENT_OBJECTS).

 

What's next?

Kernseife is currently in use (or in prior versions) in several projects. But to be very clear: There is development ongoing, but we do not have a product roadmap available.

But as it is open source, we are open to feature discussions or pull requests - or just use it for your own advantage!

entry.title: SAP Document AI — The Roadmap for Seamless Document Processing in SAP Business Suite
entry.content:
In today's digital world, efficient document processing has become an essential aspect of business operations. SAP Document AI is at the forefront of this transformation, providing innovative solutions that enhance productivity and streamline processes. Read more about the recent announcement here.

Upcoming Excellence with SAP Document AI Premium Plus 

Looking ahead, the SAP Document AI promises to simplify business document processing throughout SAP Business Suite through new, diverse and value-adding integration scenarios. For this purpose we plan to introduce the Premium Plus edition later this year. This edition harnesses the power of the latest generative AI technology, provides multi language support, offers turn-key integrations for enriched processing capabilities, instant learning capabilities, and harmonized experience across SAP’s portfolio.

 As part of the upcoming 2508 release of SAP Cloud ERP Public, the Premium Plus capabilities will enhance document efficiency with following embedded scenarios:

  • Quality Management: Automate the processing of incoming Supplier Quality Certificates to ensure quality compliance quickly and accurately.
  • Order Management: Expedite sales operations with automatic extraction of sales orders from documents in a variety of languages.
  • Inventory Management: Streamline inventory management by extracting key data from delivery notes for seamless tracking.

Users will benefit from end-to-end document processing automation, beginning with document ingestion, through AI processing, and culminating in matching with corresponding business objects within SAP Cloud ERP. All scenarios are also planned to be ported to the private edition soon [1].

For a hands-on look, watch the demo video depicting the processing of incoming supplier quality certificates in Quality Management:

Outlook and Roadmap for the entire SAP Business Suite

SAP’s vision for document processing integrates seamlessly into the current landscape and extends into the future. Future integrations of SAP Document AI include:

  • SAP SuccessFactors: Pre-configured content for high-volume HR documents like "National ID validation during New Hire Onboarding."
  • SAP Fieldglass: Efficiently upload and process Statements of Work within Services Procurement, simplifying document extraction and drafting.
  • SAP Enterprise Service Management: Specialized Document AI capabilities for business documents posting, including payment advice, into S/4HANA.
  • SAP Build Process Automation: Access to Generative AI capabilities for template-free document automation.
  • SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management: Enhanced invoice data extraction capabilities for superior invoice capture.

SAP Document AI continues to redefine document processing, offering businesses enhanced capabilities that drive efficiency, reduce errors, and empower organizations to focus on strategic tasks. Stay tuned and join our community of users on SAP Community. Stay updated on upcoming AI use case releases by exploring the SAP Roadmap Explorer. Explore more about SAP Document AI by visiting http://www.sap.com/ai/document-ai and don’t miss out on SAP's latest AI innovations at Sapphire 2025

 

Author's note:

1. SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition users can already leverage SAP Document AI capabilities featuring Generative AI, notably by subscribing to and integrating with SAP Document AI Premium Edition via SAP BTP.

2. SAP Document AI, previously known as Document Information Extraction, has been available in variety of integrative scenarios across SAP as a "base" capability e.g. SAP Concur Invoice Management, SAP Cash Application, SAP Build Process Automation, SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management and many more – check out this blog post for more details.

entry.title: Creating the Shell System for SAP Business Transformation Center: A Practical Approach
entry.content:
Lean Selective Data Transition (Lean SDT) with SAP Business Transformation Center (BTC) supports the transition of data that is initially analyzed and reduced to the amount that is relevant for continuing business in the new SAP S/4HANA system.

To use this transition path, a so-called shell copy of the source SAP ECC system is required — containing only customizing without application data, such as master or transactional data. This shell system is then converted to at least SAP S/4HANA 2023 or SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition.

In this blog, I will provide initial insights into the shell creation process and illustrate it with a practical example.

General Approach

A copy of the source system is required to create the shell (1). This can be achieved using virtualization, third-party tools, or — as in my case — a classical system copy.

Once the system copy is complete, the client designated for SAP Business Transformation Center is deleted, recreated, and copied using a client copy with a customizing-only profile (2).

Let’s take a closer look at this approach.

Initial Situation

In this example, I used an SAP ERP 6.0 EhP8 system with the System ID (SID) SRC as the source system. It contained transactional data in client 300 and was running on an AnyDB database.

To simplify the process and reduce the number of steps involved, I opted for a heterogeneous system copy, migrating directly to an SAP HANA database. The migration key required for my customer number had already been generated in advance.

For the client copy, I performed a remote client copy. Depending on the scenario, a local client copy or client export/import could also be valid alternatives.

DISCLAIMER: 
This blog shows selected steps tailored to my specific setup. When creating your own shell system, you should always follow the official SAP documentation for System Copy and Client Copy procedures, and prepare your environment accordingly. Depending on the size of the system or the connected systems, it may be advisable to consider an alternative option.

Step 1: Generate DDL Statements (Source)

To prepare for the migration of non-standard database objects, I first logged into the source system (client 000), opened transaction SE38, and executed the report SMIGR_CREATE_DDL.

In the dialog, I selected SAP HANA Database as the target and specified the installation directory before running the report.

The DDL statements have been successfully generated and stored in the specified directory.



Step 2: Export the Database Instance (Source)

First, I copied, extracted, and launched the Software Provisioning Manager (SWPM) on the source system.

On the Welcome screen, I navigated to: SAP Business Suite 7i 2016 > EHP8 for SAP ERP 6.0 ABAP > {AnyDB} > System Copy > Source System > Based on AS ABAP and then selected the Database Instance Export option.

I followed the wizard, providing the necessary inputs, such as the Export Location.

As the Target Database Type, I selected SAP HANA Database.

Next, I supplied the SQL files generated in Step 1.

Using mostly default settings, I reviewed the summary and started the export process.

After some time, the procedure completed successfully without any issues.

Finally, I copied the database export to the shell system.

Step 3: Install the Target System (Shell)

First, I transferred the Software Provisioning Manager and required installation media (SAP Kernel, SAP IGS and helper applications, SAP Host Agent, SAP HANA database and client, ...) to the shell system. I then extracted and launched the Software Provisioning Manager.

On the Welcome screen, I navigated to: SAP Business Suite 7i 2016 > EHP8 for SAP ERP 6.0 ABAP > SAP HANA Database > System Copy > Target System > Standard System > Based on AS ABAP and then selected the Standard System installation option.

I followed the wizard, retaining the same System ID (SID) as for the source system.

ADDITIONAL INFO: 
It is also possible to use a new System ID. In this specific example, however, I chose to retain the original System ID.

Next, I used the Standard System Copy / Migration (Load-Based) option.

Since the SAP HANA database was not pre-installed on the host, I configured the Software Provisioning Manager to install it as part of the standard system installation. To enable the installation, I specified localhost as the Database Host and changed the Database ID (DBSID) to HDB.

Next, I specified the Migration Export (MIGEXPORT) copied earlier from the source system.

Using mostly default settings, I reviewed the summary and started the system installation.

After some time, the procedure completed successfully without any issues. I completed the required post-processing tasks, including disabling the SAP Cloud ALM push provider, as it is not required for this system.



Step 4: Delete the Client (Shell)

Before running the client copy with the customizing profile, it is necessary to delete the existing client 300 on the shell system.

To do this, I temporarily enabled the SAP* user (login/no_automatic_user_sapstar = false) on the shell system, logged into client 300 as SAP*, and opened transaction SCC5.

The client was already pre-selected. I enabled the Delete entry from T000 checkbox and scheduled the job to run immediately in the background.

In transaction SCC3, I monitored the deletion process until it was completed.



Step 5: Recreate the Client (Shell)

As a next step, it is required to create the client 300 again in the shell system.

I logged into client 000, opened transaction SCC4, switched to Edit mode, and created the new client 300 using New Entries.

After saving, the new client was successfully created.



Step 6: Perform Remote Client Copy (Shell)

With the new client in place, I logged into client 300 as the SAP* user. I then created an RFC destination (type 3) to the source system, using the Fast serializer as a special option (CLIENTCOPY_SRCCLNT300).

In transaction SCC9, I configured a remote client copy using the profile SAP_UCSV (Customizing, User Master Records and User Variants), selected the defined RFC destination, and scheduled the job to run immediately in the background. The customizing data began to transfer to the shell system.

ADDITIONAL INFO: 
It is possible to use other customizing profiles instead of SAP_UCSV. In this example, I explicitly chose to include user master records and user variants.

Using transaction SCC3, I monitored the remote copy process until it was completed.

The shell system has been successfully created.

Summary & Next Steps

I hope, with this blog, I was able to share initial insights on the shell copy with you and provide you with a practical guide to create the system for SAP Business Transformation Center. When creating the shell copy yourself, it is essential to carefully plan your scenario and select the option that best fits your specific system landscape and project requirements.

With the shell system successfully created, it can now be converted using the standard path to SAP S/4HANA 2023 or SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition. In my case, I proceeded first with Custom Code Migration, created the Deletion Transport Request, and then converted the system to SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS03.
